title: OOP
content:
Object-oriented programming is a programming paradigm that organizes code around objects.
Objects are self-contained units that bundle data and functions together.
An object oriented program is basically a collection of objects and relationships among them.
Objects communicate or interact with each other using message passing.
This approach uses concepts like:
  • Classes (templates for objects)
  • Encapsulation (bundling data and methods)
  • Inheritance (reusing properties from other classes)
  • Polymorphism (objects taking on many forms) to create modular, reusable, extensible and generic software system.

title: Class
Encapsulation
content:
Encapsulation makes out software system modular ie. we can change one part of the code without affecting other parts of code.
Encapsulation is the process of wrapping of related data and code into a single unit which modularizes our software system.
Encapsulation also help us to achieve data hiding: the process of hiding sensitive data of the objects from the outside world.
Encapsulation refers to the bundling of data with the mechanisms or methods that operate on the data.
Encapsulation also refer to the limiting of direct access to some of that data, such as an object's components.
Essentially encapsulation prevents external code from being concerned with the internal workings of an object.
Encapsulation is java is achieved through class and access modifiers.

Data hiding is the process of hiding sensitive data of the object from the user of the object.
Data hiding is a programming concept which protects the data from direct modification by other parts of the program.
Data hiding is achieved by declaring fields as private and providing setter and getter methods for fields to access or update value. Data hiding is closely related to abstraction and encapsulation.

Advantages of encapsulation:
  • Encapsulation gives a clear and modular structure to our software system.
  • Data security: help us to make fields of a class read-only or write-only.
  • Program is well organized so easy to maintain.

title: Inheritance
content:
Inheritance let us create modules that doesnt need to be changed when new attributes or functionalities is added to the class.
Inheritance is a mechanism by which one class acquires the attributes (fields) and functionalities (methods) of another class.
This mechanism promotes code reusability and establishes a hierarchical is-a relationship between classes.
Reusability helps us to avoid duplicate code and data.
Inheritance helps us to define relationship among classes.

Kind of inheritance:
  • Single inheritance - when a class inherits another class.
  • Multi-level inheritance - when a class inherits derived class.
  • Hierarchical inheritance - when a class is inherited by many classes.

title: Abstraction
content:
Mechanism of hiding unnecessary details and showing only essential details to the user by separating interface from its impl.
Main goal of the abstraction it to reduce the complexity of the design and implementation process of software system.
Abstraction also generalises by capturing the common essense among unrelated classes.
Abstraction can be achieved through interface and abstract class.

Method = Method header + method body.
Method header is an abstraction of a block of code.
Abstract method is independent of implementation which can have many implementations.

Abstraction: Complete abstraction can be achieved through interface.
Security: Hides implementation details and only show the important details of an object.
Multiple inheritance: Can be achieved through interface because a class can implements multiple interfaces.

title: Polymorphism
content:
Polymorphism is a mechanism which allows an entity such as an variable, a method or an object to have more than one form.
Polymorphism enables us to deal with generalities and let the compiler and runtime environment to handle the specific.
Polymorphism can be achieved through method overloading, method overriding and generics.
Polymorphism is the provision of a single interface to entities of different types.
Polymorphism is the use of a single symbol to represent multiple different types.

Many methods can have one common name.
One method can accept many types of arguments.
One method header can be associated with many method body.
One type of reference variable can be used to access many type of objects.

Field level polymorphism: a reference variable of class E can point to objects of class E as well as objects of subclass of E.
Subtype polymorphism is a form of type polymorphism where a variable of a superclass refer to and object of a subclass.
Subtype polymorphism allows a single interface or symbol to represent different type of objects.
Which means code written for a superclass can be used with instances of its subclasses.
At runtime, the program calls the correct method based on the object's actual type.

Inheritance and substitutability - a subclass is considered a subtype of its superclass.
This relationship allows an object of the subclass to be substituted for an object of the superclass.
Dynamic method selection - the specific method that is executed is determined at runtime, not at compile time.
Single interface for multiple types: let us write code that operates on superclass type and work correctly with any of its subclasses.

title: Overloading
content:
Process of writing multiple methods with the same name in the same class is called method overloading.
Method-overloading is a kind of static polymorphism.
  • Method overloading increases the readability of the program.
  • Avoids the need to memorize multiple method names.
  • Method overloading makes our code more generic.
  • Method overloading support clean code.
  • The complexity of the code is reduced.
  • Compiler binds the method call with an approriate method body.
Method overloading is achieved by:
  • Changing the number of arguments.
  • Changing the data type of the arguments.

title: Overriding
content:
Method-overriding is the process of redefining a parent class instance method into its child class is known as method overriding. An instance method defined in the parent class is redefined into its child class to give specific implementation of the parent class method. Referenced object type, not the reference variable type determines which method will be called. When we create a parent class reference variable and assign a child class object then calling an overridden method on that variable will call the child class behaviour.

  • Final methods can not be overridden.
  • Static methods can not be overridden.
  • Private methods can not be overridden.
  • Child class method is called overriding method.
  • Parent class method is called overridden method.
  • Overridden methods are dynamically dispatched.
  • Finalize method can be overridden to free system resources.
  • Overridden parent class method can be called using super keyword.
  • Runtime polymorphism can be achieved through method overriding.
  • Abstract methods can only be overridden by the first concrete class.
  • The access level cannot be more restrictive than the overridden method.
  • Override annotation denotes that the method overrides the base class method.

title: Binding
content:
Dynamic binding is a process in which a call to overridden method is resolved at the runtime rather than compile time.
Which means that the code to be executed for a specific method call is not known until runtime.
Dynamic dispatch is used when multiple classes contain different implementations of the same method.
Dynamic binding (dispatch) is the process of linking function call to a specific sequence of code (function) at run-time.
Dynamic dispatch is a mechanism for selecting the function to be executed from various function alternatives at the runtime.

title: Association
content:
Association represents a relationship where one object is linked to another object.
Association describes relationships between classes.
The association can be one-way or two-way. Which implies that two classes are related, but neither class owns the other.
Associations relate entities that can exist independently of each other - such as authors and books.

title: Aggregation
content:
Aggregation is a special form of association where one object contains another object.
However the contained object can exist independently of the container.
Lifetime of the contained object is not dependent on the lifetime of the container.
Aggregation represents has-a relationship between objects.
Composition represents part-of relationship between objects.
An aggregation is a collection or the gathering of things together.

Aggregation - A has an instance of B, B can exists without A.
Composition - A has an instance of B, B cannot exists without A.

title: Composition
content:
Composition is a stronger form of association where one object contains another object and the contained object cannot exist independently of the container. The lifetime of the contained object depends on the lifetime of the container. In composition if owning object is garbage collected the owned object will also be which is not the case in aggregation.

Composition means the child entity (order items) is part of a whole and cannot exist independently of the parent entity (order). The child entity is contained in the parent entity and can only be accessed via the parent entity.

Both composition and aggregation express a has-a relationship.
Composition expresses a strong has-a relationship (whole-part ownership).
Aggregation expresses a weak has-a relationship (association without ownership).
Both composition and inheritance used to solve the same problem that is code reuse.
Process of building complex objects from simpler ones is called object composition.
Use inheritance only when you want both hierarchical reuse of code and subtype polymorphism.

title: Dependency
content:
Dependency injection generally means passing a dependent object as a parameter to a method rather than having the method create the dependent object. Dependency injection is a software design pattern that allow us to develop loosely-coupled software. Dependecy injection allows us to depends more on abstraction rather than the concrete implementation, which promotes loosely-coupled architecture, flexibility and pluggability within our code. What it means in practice is that the method does not have a direct dependency on a particular implementation, any implementation that meets the requirements can be passed as a parameter.

Dependency injection is a programming technique where an object or function receives its dependencies (other objects or functions it needs) from an external source, rather than creating them itself. This approach, which is a form of Inversion of Control, makes code more flexible, maintainable, and testable by decoupling components and allowing them to be replaced or mocked easily.

title: SOLID
content:
Solid principle is a set of guidelines for writing code that makes it easier to manage, extend and debug.
Solid principle help us break big problem into smaller easier to handle parts.
Solid reduces dependencies on code so changes in one area won't break another.
Solid reduces tight coupling, making code more flexible and easier to maintain.

title: Design Patterns
content:
Design patterns are reusable solutions to commonly occurring problems in software design.
Design patterns are solutions or techniques to avoid code design problem.
Design patterns help us write robust, clean, modular and extensible code.
Design patterns provide a template for solving design issues in various contexts.
Design patterns help us write robust, clean, modular, maintainable, scalable and understandable and extensible code.

Design patterns are divided into three categories:
  • Creational patterns - Deals with object creation.
  • Structural patterns - Deals with object composition.
  • Behavioral Patterns - Deals with object interaction and responsibility.
Advantages
Reusability Provide a reusable solution to common software design problems which saves developers from reinventing the wheel whenever they encounter a new problem. Instead, they can use a tried and tested pattern to solve the problem. Maintainability Promote clean, organized and maintainable code which makes it easier for developers to maintain and modify the code as the software evolves. Rapid-developement Provide a standard way of solving common software design problems which saves time and effort in the desing and development process, allowing developers to focus on other aspects of the software.
Creational: Singleton, Builder, Factory Method, Abstract Factory and Prototype.
Structural: Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Flyweight and Proxy.
Behavioral: CoR, Command, Iterator, Mediator, Memento, Observer, State, Strategy, Template and Visitor.

title: Adapter
content:
Adapter pattern is a structural design pattern that allows objects with incompatible interfaces to work together.
Adapter acts as a bridge between two incompatible interfaces.
Adapter pattern converts the interface of a class into another interface that the client expects.
Adapter lets classes work together that couldn’t otherwise because of incompatible interfaces.
Adapter catches calls for one object and transforms them to format and interface recognizable by the second object.
Adapter pattern should be used when:
  • There is an existing class and its interface does not match the one you need.
  • We need to connect systems or components that were not built to work together.
Components:
  • Target: The interface the client expects.
  • Adaptee: The existing class that needs adapting.
  • Adapter: The class that implements the target interface and internally uses or adapts the adaptee.
Pros and Cons:
  • Reusability of existing functionality.
  • Keeps existing code untouched.
  • Code can become complex if many adapters are introduced.
  • Sometimes it's better to refactor than use adapters excessively.

title: Facade
content:
Facade pattern is a structural design pattern that provides a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem.
A complex interface to the client is considered as a fault in the design of a system.
Facade defines a higher-level interface that makes the subsystem easier to use.
Facade pattern makes a complex interface easier to use using a Facade class.

When-to use:
  • When you want to simplify the usage of a complex system.
  • When there are multiple subsystems that need to be integrated.
  • To decouple clients from the internal components of a system.
  • When you want to create a layered architecture.
Advantages:
  • Simplifies complex interfaces.
  • Makes the system easier to use.
  • Promotes loose coupling.
  • Improves code readability.

title: Composite
content:
Composite: kind of structural design pattern used when we want to treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly.
This is especially useful when you deal with hierarchical tree structures, such as file systems, DOM tree or UI components.
Composite lets clients treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly.

When-to use:
  • When we want to compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies.
Components:
  • Component: interface or abstract class for all objects in the hierarchy, both composite and leaf.
  • Leaf: represents individual objects (end nodes) which implements the base Component.
  • Composite: a container that holds child Components.
  • Client: uses the Component interface to interact with objects in the hierarchy uniformly.
Use cases:
  • GUI frameworks (Swing or HTML DOM trees)
  • Organization hierarchy (employees, managers)
  • File system (files and directories)
  • Document structures (paragraphs, sections, characters)

title: Proxy
content:
Proxy pattern is a structural design pattern that provides a proxy for other objects to control access to them.
Proxy implements the same interface as the real object and can add behavior like access control, logging, caching, lazy loading, etc.
A proxy is a wrapper or agent object that the client calls to access the real serving object behind the scenes. Extra functionalities can be provided into proxy such as caching the operations on the actual object or checking pre-conditions before calling operations on the actual object. A proxy functions as an interface to something else.

When-to use:
  • When we need to perform additional operations (lazy loading, caching, access control) when accessing an object.
Components:
  • Subject (Interface) – Declares common interface for RealSubject and Proxy.
  • RealSubject – The actual object that does the work.
  • Proxy – Controls access to RealSubject, implements the same interface.
Real-world example:
  • Java RMI – Uses proxy to call remote objects.
  • Spring AOP – Uses proxies for method interception.
  • Hibernate – Lazy-loaded entities are proxies.
  • Security managers – Proxy access to critical resources.
  • Credit card - Acts as a proxy for our bank account.

title: Flyweight
content:
Flyweight pattern is a structural design pattern that reduces memory uses by sharing similar objects.
Flyweight pattern uses a factory to manage these shared objects and ensures that each object is created only once.
When we need large number of similar objects then creating and storing each object separately can take too much memory.

Components:
  • Flyweight interface – Declares methods that accept extrinsic state.
  • ConcreteFlyweight – Implements Flyweight and stores intrinsic state.
  • FlyweightFactory – Manages a pool of flyweight objects.
  • Client – Maintains or computes extrinsic state and passes it to the flyweight.
Pros and cons:
  • Reduces memory usage by sharing objects.
  • Increases performance when creating many similar objects.
  • Makes code more complex.
  • Requires careful separation of intrinsic and extrinsic state.

title: Decorator
content:
Decorator is a structural design pattern that allows us to add new behavior or functionality to objects dynamically without altering their structure or modifying their source code. This is achieved by creating decorator classes that wrap the original object and add new behaviours or attributes. Decorators provide a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality. Decorator design pattern allows behaviours to be added to individual objects, either statically or dynamically without affecting the behaviour of other objects from the same class.

When-to use:
  • When we want to add responsibilities to individual objects, not an entire class.
  • When we want to keep classes open for extension but closed for modification.
Components:
  • Component: The interface or abstract class defining the operations.
  • Concrete component: The original object that we want to extend.
  • Decorator: Implements the same interface and has a reference to a Component object.
  • Concrete Decorators: Add new behaviors before or after delegating the request to the wrapped component.
Advantages:
  • Adheres to SRP and OCP.
  • Greater flexibility than static inheritance.
  • Can add or remove responsibilities at runtime.
Real-world examples:
  • Java IO Streams.
  • Spring's Transactional, Cacheable can be seen as decorators.
  • Spring Framework - Middleware or Interceptors decorate request and response handling.

title: Observer
content:
Observer pattern is a behavioral design pattern that defines a one-to-many dependency between objects.
In this pattern an object known as subject maintains a list of its dependent objects known as observers.
When the subject's state changes, all objects that depend on it are notified and updated automatically by subject.

When-to use:
  • Use Observer when you want to automatically notify multiple objects of state changes.
  • Great for event-driven systems, GUIs, distributed messaging, and real-time updates.
  • Think of it as a push-based notification system for objects.
Components:
  • Subject – The core object being observed. Maintains a list of observers.
  • Observer – Interface that defines the update method.
  • ConcreteSubject – Implements subject and notifies observers.
  • ConcreteObserver – Implements observer and responds to state changes.

title: Visitor
content:
Visitor is a behavioral design pattern that lets us add new operations to existing object structures without modifying their classes.
Useful in scenario when an object structure is stable but we frequently need to extend its behaviours with new operations.
Visitor pattern helps separate algorithms from the objects on which they operate.
Visitor pattern aligns with open-closed concepts.

Components:
  • Visitor – Interface or abstract class for visiting elements.
  • ConcreteVisitor – Implements operations on concrete elements.
  • Element – Interface that defines accept(visitor)
  • ConcreteElement – Implements accept(visitor)
  • Client – Traverses object structure and applies visitor.
Real-world examples:
  • Filesystem utilities – Backup, search, indexing tools can visit folders and files.
  • Game engines – Collision detection or rendering can be implemented as visitors over game entities.
  • AST traversal– Different visitors (semantic analyzer, code generator) operate on the same tree nodes.
  • Document converters – HTML, PDF, MD converters can visit document elements (paragraphs, images, links).
Pros and cons:
  • Add new operations without changing existing object structures.
  • Encourages separation of concerns (data vs behavior).
  • Makes operations extensible and easy to manage.
  • Adding a new element (type) requires updating all visitors.
  • Involves double dispatch – one call from client to element, one from element to visitor.

title: Mediator
content:
Mediator is a behavioral design pattern that promotes loose coupling between objects by centralizing communication between them. Instead of objects referring to each other directly, they communicate through a mediator object. This makes the system easier to maintain, modify, and scale, especially as the number of objects increases. Mediator pattern focuses on simplifying communication between objects by managing and coordinating their interations through a mediator. Mediator pattern is used for coordinating communication while the proxy pattern is used for controlling access.

Components:
  • Mediator interface: Declares the communication contract used by components.
  • ConcreteMediator: Implements coordination logic and knows all components (colleagues).
  • Colleague: Individual objects that communicate via mediator. Knows about the mediator but not other colleagues.
Pros and Cons:
  • Promotes low coupling between components.
  • Simplifies maintenance and enhances reusability.
  • Centralizes complex communications.
  • Mediator can become a God object if it gets too complex.
  • Too much logic inside the mediator can lead to poor scalability.

title: Iterator
content:
Iterator pattern is a behavioral design pattern that provides a way to access the elements of a collection (like a list, tree or graph) sequentially without exposing its underlying representation. Iterator pattern is used to traverse elements in a container.
Intent is to provide a standard way to traverse through a collection of objects without exposing its internal structure.
Iterator pattern decouples container traversal algorithms from containers.

Components:
  • Iterator (Interface) – declares the interface for accessing elements.
  • ConcreteIterator – implements the Iterator interface and keeps track of the current position.
  • Aggregate (Interface) – declares a method to return an iterator.
  • ConcreteAggregate – implements the method to return an iterator instance.
Advantages:
  • Separates collection traversal logic from collection data structure.
  • Uniform traversal for various types of collections.
  • Supports multiple iterations simultaneously.

title: COR
content:
CoR design pattern is a behavioral design pattern that decouples sender and receiver of a request by connecting multiple handlers in a chain. The request is then passed along the chain until it is processed by one of the handlers. When-to use: when we have multiple objects that can handle a request but the specific object that handles it is determined at runtime.

Components:
Handler interface: Declares a method for handling requests and a reference to the next handler.
ConcreteHandler: Handles requests it is responsible for or forwards them to the next handler.
Client: Sends the request to the first handler in the chain.
Workflow:
A client sends a request to the first handler in the chain, if the handler can handle it, it does.
Otherwise, it forwards the request to the next handler in the chain.
This continues until the request is handled or the end of the chain is reached.
